title: Variables & Types
description: Master C++ fundamental types and casting — knowing when to use int vs long long, how to cast safely, and how nullptr works prevents subtle bugs during interviews.

sections.title: Declaring variables
sections.explanation: C++ is statically typed — every variable needs a type at declaration. The five types you'll use constantly: **int**, **long long**, **double**, **string**, and **bool**. Use `auto` when the type is obvious from the right-hand side.

exercise.prompt: Declare five variables: `count` (int 42), `big` (long long 1000000000000), `ratio` (double 3.14), `name` (string "alice"), and `found` (bool true). Print each variable on its own line using cout.

validation.message: Each variable should print its value on a separate line
Declare each variable with its correct type

exercise.hint: Assign the literals directly: count = 42, big = 1000000000000LL, ratio = 3.14, name = "alice", found = true.

sections.title: Type conversion with static_cast
sections.explanation: In interviews, you often need to convert between types — integer division to floating-point, char to int for indexing, etc. Use **static_cast<T>(expr)** for safe, explicit conversions. Also know **stoi()** / **stoll()** for string-to-number and **to_string()** for number-to-string.

exercise.prompt: Divide int `a = 7` by int `b = 2` using static_cast to get a double result. Then convert the string "99" to an int with stoi, and convert int 256 to a string with to_string. Print all three results.

validation.message: Check your casts — the division should produce 3.5, stoi gives 99, to_string gives "256"
Use static_cast for the division, stoi for string-to-int, and to_string for int-to-string

exercise.hint: Use static_cast<double>(a) / b for the division. num = stoi(s) and text = to_string(256).

sections.title: nullptr and null checks
sections.explanation: In C++ interviews, **nullptr** represents a null pointer — you'll use it constantly with tree nodes and linked lists. Always check `if (ptr == nullptr)` or simply `if (!ptr)` before dereferencing. Never use the old C macro `NULL`; interviewers expect modern C++ style with `nullptr`.

exercise.prompt: Create a simple linked list with values 10 -> 20 -> 30. Traverse it with a while loop, printing each value separated by spaces. Use nullptr to check the end of the list.

validation.message: Traverse the linked list printing each value followed by a space
Use nullptr for the null check and curr->next to advance

exercise.hint: Check while (curr != nullptr), then advance with curr = curr->next.

sections.title: const and common constants
sections.explanation: Use **const** for values that should never change. In interviews, you'll define constants for MOD arithmetic, infinity, and direction arrays. `INT_MAX` and `INT_MIN` from `<climits>` are available with bits/stdc++.h. For larger ranges, use `LLONG_MAX`.

exercise.prompt: Define three constants: `MOD` as 1e9 + 7 (int), `INF` as INT_MAX, and `MAX_SIZE` as 1000. Print each constant, then print 123456789 % MOD and whether 99 < INF (as boolalpha).

validation.message: Check your constant values — MOD is 1e9+7, INF is INT_MAX (2147483647)
Use const for all three declarations and INT_MAX for infinity

exercise.hint: MOD = 1e9 + 7, INF = INT_MAX, MAX_SIZE = 1000. 123456789 % MOD equals itself since it's smaller than MOD.
